title: NDI Rules & FDI Framework
content:
Session 6: 
Dropti Devi v Union of India [2016 | SC |  2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Dropti Devi, the mother of the detenue Raj Kumar Aggarwal, challenged his preventive detention under COFEPOSA 
ordered by the Joint Secretary, Government of India.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On 17 February 2009, the ED conducted raids at Ambika Electronics, recovering about ‚āĻ8.9 lakhs in cash, incriminating 
documents, and seizing the detenue‚Äôs passport.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Further searches were carried out at the residence of his brother and at Bhagwati Electronics.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The detenue was interrogated overnight and a statement was recorded, which he later retracted, followed by further 
statements in May 2009.  
194 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The material disclosed continuous hawala transactions, with the last transaction dated 24 April 2009, leading to the 
detention order to prevent further foreign exchange violations. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the activities constituted hawala transactions 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether detention under COFEPOSA was justified 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether FEMA is materially less stringent than FERA regarding forex control 
 
Rule:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Hawala is an informal, unregulated system of transferring money. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No physical movement of money across borders. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Operates through trust-based networks, without paperwork, banking channels. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Though functionally similar to banking, hawala completely bypasses authorised foreign exchange mechanisms, resulting in 
depletion of forex reserves. 
 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Hawala and Depletion of Foreign Exchange 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The detenue‚Äôs activities involved informal, unregulated movement of foreign exchange. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Bypassed authorised channels under FEMA. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Resulted in depletion of forex, unlike legitimate commercial inflows/outflows. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC treated it as hawala + smuggling + depletion of forex ‚Üí serious threats to economic stability. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA vs FERA ‚ÄĒ Substantive Continuity 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Petitioners argued that since FEMA decriminalised violations (unlike FERA), preventive detention is 
unconstitutional ‚Äď REJECTED 
‚ěĘ‚Äč While penalties differ, substantive control over foreign exchange remains rigorous. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Government control over forex has not been diluted. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Conservation and augmentation of forex is as vital under FEMA as under FERA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Preventive Detention Without Criminal Offence 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Preventive detention is precautionary, not punitive. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Aims to keep the detenu out of circulation (Poonam Lata v M.L. Wadhawan). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Is constitutionally distinct from arrest/prosecution. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Constitutional Challenge (Articles 14, 19, 21) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Argument: Preventive detention cannot be justified where no punitive detention is provided. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Court‚Äôs response: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
COFEPOSA‚Äôs constitutional validity was already upheld in Amratlal Prajivandas. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Repeal of FERA does not erase national interest in forex conservation. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Economic conditions (booming trade, forex reserves) do not eliminate vulnerability. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The detenue‚Äôs activities constituted hawala transactions, involving informal and unauthorised foreign exchange dealings. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Such activities are prejudicial to the conservation and augmentation of foreign exchange. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Preventive detention under COFEPOSA is constitutionally valid, even after repeal of FERA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč An act need not be a criminal offence under FEMA to justify preventive detention. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The detention order was upheld. 
 
Vijay Karia v Prysmian [2020 | SC | 3 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Shareholders‚Äô Agreement & Buy-out Clause 
195 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Clause 23.4 of the agreement provided a default-based buy-out mechanism ‚Üí upon an Event of Default not being 
rectified within the stipulated period, the Non-Defaulting Party could: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Require the Defaulting Party to sell its shares at a 10% discount to Fair Market Value, or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Buy shares at a 10% premium, at the option of the non-defaulting party. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arbitration resulted in an award in favour of the Italian company (Prysmian) ‚Üí directed Vijay Karia (resident in India) to 
sell his shares in Ravin to the foreign party at a 10% discount to fair market value. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Karia resisted enforcement of the foreign award in India, arguing that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The buy-out at a discount violated Rule 9(3) read with Rule 21(2)(b)(ii)/(iii) of the Foreign Exchange Management 
(Non-Debt Instrument) Rules, 2019. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Under these Rules: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Transfer of equity instruments by a resident to a non-resident must comply with pricing 
guidelines. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
The price cannot be less than fair market value as determined by a registered valuer. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A 10% discount, therefore, allegedly caused a net foreign exchange outflow, since India would receive less 
than full value. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether an arbitral award directing a buy-out of shares at a discount, allegedly in violation of FEMA / NDI Rules, breaches 
the fundamental policy of Indian law, thereby justifying refusal of enforcement under Section 48(2)(b). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether a FEMA violation renders the underlying contract void, or the arbitral award unenforceable. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 48(2)(b): Enforcement may be refused if contrary to public policy of India. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Renusagar standard: ‚ÄúFundamental policy‚ÄĚ means breach of a legal principle so basic that it cannot be 
compromised. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA & NDI Rules 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rule 9(3), FEMA (NDI) Rules, 2019: Pricing guidelines must be complied with. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rule 21(2)(b): Price for resident-to-non-resident transfer cannot be below fair market value. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FERA v FEMA Distinction 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 47 of FERA, 1973 (now repealed): Catch-all provision penalising contracts entered into to evade foreign 
exchange law. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA does not contain a corresponding provision voiding contracts. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Key Precedents 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Renusagar Power Co. v GE ‚Äď FEMA/FERA cannot be used as a shield to resist enforcement. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Cruz City v Unitech ‚Äď FEMA violations do not ipso facto make awards unenforceable. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Docomo v Tata (contrast): net foreign exchange outflow scrutinised. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA vs FERA: Structural Shift 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FERA ‚Üí policing regime (criminal sanctions, voiding tendency). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA ‚Üí management and facilitation regime. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 47 of FERA, often relied upon to argue contracts were void, no longer exists. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, a transaction violating FEMA is not void ‚Üí at most, it is irregular or remediable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Rectifiable Breach under FEMA 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even assuming that the NDI Rules mandate sale at not less than fair market value, and the award directs sale at a 
discount, the breach is rectifiable, because: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RBI may grant post-facto approval, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RBI may direct compliance (e.g., insist on market value), 
196 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
RBI may condone the breach. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, neither the agreement, nor the award, becomes void or unenforceable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Fundamental Policy of Indian Law 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ‚ÄúFundamental policy‚ÄĚ refers to core legal principles, basic values underlying India‚Äôs legal system ‚Üí not every 
statutory infraction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA violations are regulatory, not foundational + are capable of compromise and correction. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, resistance to enforcement on this ground fails. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Foreign Exchange Inflow Consideration 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Crucially, this case involved foreign exchange inflow, not depletion ‚Üí Italian company was buying shares in an 
Indian company. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even if discounted, there was no smuggling, no depletion of reserves, no threat to India‚Äôs economic fabric ‚Üí 
distinguished the case from contexts involving illegal outflows or smuggling. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Response to Dropti Devi Argument 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Appellants relied on Dropti Devi, arguing FEMA violations are serious and form public policy ‚Äď REJECTED 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Dropti Devi was decided in the COFEPOSA preventive detention context ‚Üí observations on FEMA‚Äôs importance 
were context-specific ‚áí cannot extrapolate that every FEMA violation = public policy breach. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Importance of foreign exchange ‚Č† every FEMA violation is a fundamental policy breach. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč ‚ÄúFaustian Bargain‚ÄĚ & Party Conduct ‚Üí parties entering commercial arrangements, enjoying benefits, then invoking FEMA 
to escape liabilities. 
 
Conclusion:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The foreign arbitral award does not violate the fundamental policy of Indian law ‚Üí enforcement cannot be refused under 
Section 48(2)(b). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA ‚Č† FERA ‚Üí no voiding of contracts under FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Rectifiable breaches do not affect enforceability. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RBI, not courts, is the appropriate authority to address FEMA non-compliance. 
 
Banyan Tree Growth Capital LLC v Axiom Cordages Ltd. & Ors. [2020 | BHC | 1 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Banyan Tree Growth Capital LLC, a Mauritius-based investment fund, invested about USD 7.5 million in Axiom Cordages 
Ltd., acquiring an 11.25% stake under a Share Subscription Agreement dated 12 September 2008.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Axiom was promoted by Responsive Industries Ltd. and Wellknown/Business Ventures LLP.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The investment was backed by a Put Option Deed and an Escrow Agreement to secure Banyan Tree‚Äôs exit if an IPO or 
merger failed.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In 2014, Wellknown breached the escrow arrangements and later claimed the put option was illegal under Indian law.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arbitration under SIAC Rules resulted in an award dated 15 January 2019 upholding the put option‚Äôs validity and granting 
damages, which Banyan Tree sought to enforce in India. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Put Option Deed was inadequately stamped, rendering it illegal? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Put Option Deed was unenforceable under SCRA, 1956? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the Put Option Deed was unenforceable under FEMA, 1999? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether enforcement of the Award was contrary to the fundamental policy of Indian law / public policy? 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Arbitration & Conciliation Act, 1996 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Sections 47‚Äď49: Limited grounds for refusing enforcement of foreign awards 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 48: Public policy narrowly construed 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Maharashtra Stamp Act, 1958, Section 35: Once admitted in evidence, stamping cannot be questioned 
197 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SCRA, 1956 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Objective: Prevent undesirable speculation in securities 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 18A: Validity of derivatives traded on recognised exchanges 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 16 notifications (1969, 2013) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA, 1999 ‚Äď regulates mode of performance, not validity of contracts 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA (TISPRO) Regulations, 2000 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 5(1): Optionality clauses 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 10: FMV pricing guidelines 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Stamping Objection 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under Clause 7.3(e) of the Put Option Deed, stamp duty was expressly payable by Responsive and Wellknown.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
For over a decade, the respondents proceeded on the footing that the document was validly stamped and 
raised no objection before the arbitral tribunal, even though the contract was governed by Indian law. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Once the arbitral tribunal admitted the Put Option Deed in evidence and adjudicated rights under it, Section 35 of 
the Maharashtra Stamp Act was triggered.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Relying on Javer Chand v. Pukhraj Surana ‚Üí admission of a document in evidence is conclusive, and its 
stamping cannot later be questioned at any stage of the proceedings. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Distinguished SMS Tea Estates and Garware Wall Ropes ‚Üí those cases concerned Section 11 jurisdiction at a 
pre-arbitral stage.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
In contrast, enforcement proceedings arise after adjudication, and entertaining stamping objections at 
this stage would undermine finality and reopen factual issues ‚Üí expressly barred under Section 48. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the stamping objection was rejected both on statutory finality and estoppel by conduct. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SCRA and Alleged Illegality 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Legislative intent ‚Üí to prevent undesirable speculation in securities, not to prohibit legitimate capital-raising 
arrangements.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Put Option Deed is NOT speculative ‚Üí Banyan Tree‚Äôs investment was aimed at capital formation, with 
the Put Option serving merely as a protective exit mechanism. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even assuming the applicability of the 1969 notification under Section 16 SCRA ‚Üí the notification targeted 
speculative forward trading, not shareholder exit rights embedded in subscription agreements.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Put Option was neither traded nor tradable on a stock exchange, nor did it contemplate market 
speculation. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SEBI Notification dated 3 October 2013 ‚Üí provided explicit statutory recognition to shareholder agreements 
containing put options, including those entered into prior to the notification.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Importantly, the notification expressly preserved existing rights, making it clear that no retrospective 
invalidation was intended. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Further, invoking Edelweiss v. Percept, the Court rejected the argument that a put option constitutes a derivative 
under Section 18A ‚Äď it merely validates exchange-traded derivatives and does not invalidate private contractual 
options between shareholders. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the arbitral tribunal‚Äôs view that the Put Option Deed was valid under the SCRA was held to be not merely 
plausible, but firmly grounded in law. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA Objection 
‚ěĘ‚Äč On FEMA, the Court adopted the reasoning of Vijay Karia, drawing a sharp distinction between: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Illegality of contracts, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulatory compliance in performance involving foreign exchange. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FEMA does not render contracts void; it merely regulates how foreign exchange transactions are to be carried out. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The Put Option Deed did not guarantee assured returns and was exercisable only upon contingent 
condition + the pricing mechanism complied with FEMA valuation norms, and any excess over FMV was 
structured to remain in India. 
198 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Consequently, even assuming some regulatory infraction, such violation could at best attract penalties under 
FEMA but could not invalidate the contract or bar enforcement of a foreign award. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Public Policy / Fundamental Policy 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Finally, applying Renusagar as reaffirmed in Shri Lal Mahal, the Court examined whether enforcement would 
violate: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Fundamental policy of Indian law, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Interests of India, or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Justice or morality. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Having upheld the legality of the Put Option Deed under stamping law, SCRA, and FEMA, the Court concluded 
that none of these thresholds were met.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The respondents‚Äô objections were essentially contractual and regulatory, not matters implicating India‚Äôs core legal 
or economic policy. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Put Option Deed was valid, legal, and enforceable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Objections based on stamp duty, SCRA, and FEMA were rejected. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Award was not contrary to the public policy or fundamental policy of Indian law. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The foreign award dated 15 January 2019 was declared binding under Section 46 and enforceable as a decree under Part II of 
the Arbitration Act, 1996. 
 
Session 7 
GPE (India) Limited v Twarit Consultancy Services Pvt Ltd 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč GPE (India) Ltd and GPE JV1 Ltd (Mauritian investors) invested ~‚āĻ125 crore in Haldia Coke & Chemicals Pvt Ltd under 
an SSHA, subscribing to equity and preference shares ‚Üí provided multiple exit options, including a put option assuring 
24% IRR, unlinked to fair market value (guaranteed return‚Äďlike). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Subsequently, SPAs and Letter Agreements fixed an exit price of ‚āĻ200 crore payable in 14 tranches.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Only ‚āĻ5 crore was paid; default followed and shares were never transferred.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The SPAs contained an SIAC arbitration clause (seat: Singapore; governing law: Indian law). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Investors commenced arbitration claiming ‚āĻ401 crore (24% IRR) or alternatively ‚āĻ195 crore ‚Üí held the IRR clause to be a 
penalty, relying on Fateh Chand v Balkishan Das, and awarded ‚āĻ195 crore plus interest as reasonable compensation. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Enforcement was sought before the MHC, where respondents resisted on public policy grounds, arguing the agreements 
violated FEMA and the FEMA (Transfer of Security) Regulations, 2000 by providing a guaranteed return. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether contractual clauses guaranteeing an exit at a fixed IRR violate FEMA and the Security Transfer Regulations? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether such violation renders the SSHA and SPAs void or the arbitral award unenforceable as being contrary to the 
fundamental policy of Indian law? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether damages awarded for breach of the SPAs require prior approval of the RBI; and whether the Dhc‚Äôs ruling in NTT 
Docomo v Tata Sons applied to the facts of the case? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Dealing in securities under FEMA = capital account transaction. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 5 of the FEMA (Transfer of Security) Regulations, 2000 allows non-resident purchase of shares under the FDI 
automatic route, subject to Schedule I conditions. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč At the time of investment, the share price must be not less than fair market value (FMV) ‚Üí operates as a floor price. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Exit pricing rule (post-23 May 2014 amendment): Exit options are permitted, but without RBI approval, the exit price 
cannot exceed FMV ‚Üí operates as a ceiling. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulatory rationale: Equity investment entails uncapped upside and uncapped downside risk. 
199 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Guaranteed returns: Assured IRR or fixed returns undermine the equity character and effectively convert the investment 
into debt, which is more tightly regulated. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Enforcement of foreign awards: The concept of ‚Äúfundamental policy of Indian law‚ÄĚ must be interpreted narrowly 
(Renusagar). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA violations: A mere breach of FEMA does not automatically void a transaction. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Post-Vijay Karia position: FEMA is a managerial/regulatory statute, not a penal one; rectifiable or condonable violations 
cannot amount to a breach of the fundamental policy of Indian law. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč It is not the rights and obligations under the SSHA that are per se contrary to FEMA, but the actual transfer of shares at a 
price exceeding the fair market value without RBI approval that may be impermissible.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Following Vijay Karia, the SSHAs and SPAs were not void BUT enforceability remains subject to compliance with 
FEMA and, where required, RBI approval. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Equity investment under FEMA must genuinely involve both upside and downside risk.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč A clause guaranteeing a minimum IRR of 24% departs from this principle and resembles a debt-like return. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč  While none of the exit options under the SSHA were actually exercised and there was therefore no foreign 
exchange outflow under the SSHA, the structure of the clauses indicated that an exit at a price higher than fair 
market value would have required RBI approval. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The arbitral tribunal had not enforced the guaranteed IRR clause ‚Üí INSTEAD ‚Üí it had awarded damages equivalent to 
the unpaid sale consideration under the SPAs, which amounted to INR 195 crore.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The appropriate measure of damages was the difference between the agreed price and the market value of the shares 
at the time of breach, which in this case was zero. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč BUT, the Court departed from the DHC‚Äôs reasoning in NTT Docomo.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč While Docomo had held that RBI approval was not required for payment of damages awarded for breach of a share 
purchase obligation, the MHC held that where damages are equivalent to the entire unpaid sale consideration and 
the market value of the shares at the time of breach is nil, prior RBI approval is required ‚Üí payment of such 
damages could result in foreign exchange outflow, depending on whether the amount is repatriated outside India. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč BUT this requirement of RBI approval does not arise from a violation of fundamental public policy.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
FEMA violations are rectifiable, and RBI retains discretion to condone or regulate such transactions ‚Üí 
enforcement of the award could not be refused outright. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Investors appealed the MHC order making enforcement of the foreign award conditional on prior RBI approval ‚Üí argued: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The award did not enforce a guaranteed return or exit option under FEMA. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč It awarded damages for breach of an independent contractual obligation to purchase shares. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such damages operate in personam, and are conceptually distinct from a transfer of securities. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč RBI clarified that damages for breach of contract are not capital account transactions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such payments qualify as current account transactions under s.5 FEMA read with the CAT Rules, 2000 ‚Üí 
generally liberalised and do not require prior approval unless expressly restricted. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SC accepted RBI‚Äôs classification of damages as a CAT + set aside the requirement of prior RBI approval. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Held that FEMA conditions cannot be imported into the execution of a compensatory arbitral award. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rejection of guaranteed return argument: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Payment of damages for breach of a share purchase obligation cannot be equated with enforcement of an 
impermissible guaranteed return or exit mechanism. 
 
Aapico Hitech Public Company Limited v Sakthi Auto Component Limited 
Facts: 
200 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Pursuant to the JV arrangement, Aapico Group agreed to invest USD 100 million, of which USD 50 million was equity and 
USD 50 million was advanced as debt under loan agreements ‚Üí secured by personal guarantees given by Dr. Mahalingam, 
the founder and controlling mind of SACL, and by ABT Auto. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč In 2018, SG-USA faced a severe financial crisis ‚Üí Aapico Group infused an additional USD 65 million, of which USD 25 
million was equity, raising Aapico Group‚Äôs shareholding in SGAH to 49.99%, and the remaining amount was provided as 
debt under an amended and restated loan agreement dated 29 September 2018, which was embedded in the SHA ‚Üí in 
exchange, ABT pledged its 50.01% shareholding in SGAH in favour of Aapico Group by a charge dated 1 October 2018. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On 4 June 2019, Aapico Group alleged defaults under the loan agreements and assumed complete control of the board of 
SGAH ‚Üí, on 15 August 2019, Aapico Group enforced the pledge, appropriated ABT‚Äôs 50.01% shares, and thereby became 
the 100% equity holder of SGAH + indirectly acquired 77.04% shareholding in SACL. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Claiming that despite becoming the sole shareholder of SGAH and the majority indirect shareholder of SACL, it was being 
prevented from exercising its control and management rights under the SHA ‚Üí invoked arbitration before the SIAC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On 6 October 2021, the SIAC tribunal passed an award in favour of them, recognising their rights to control and manage 
SACL in accordance with the SHA ‚Üí thereafter approached the MHC seeking enforcement of the foreign award under 
Sections 47 and 49 of the Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996. 
 
Issues:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the foreign award satisfied the conditions under Sections 47‚Äď49 of the Arbitration Act to be enforced as a decree in 
India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether violations of FEMA and the Foreign Exchange Management (Transfer or Issue of Any Foreign Security) 
Regulations, 2004 were curable in nature. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether enforcement of the award could be refused on grounds of fraud, suppression of material facts, and contravention 
of RBI regulations, attracting Section 48(2)(b) (public policy of India). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether undervaluation of shares and loss of foreign exchange to the Indian exchequer rendered the award unenforceable. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 47‚Äď49, Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996: Governing enforcement of foreign awards. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 48(2)(b): Enforcement may be refused if contrary to the public policy of India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA and Foreign Exchange Management (Transfer or Issue of Any Foreign Security) Regulations, 2004: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 16: Transfer of shares of JV/WOS outside India must be at fair value. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Regulation 18: Pledge of shares of JV/WOS/step-down subsidiaries requires prior RBI approval. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Rule 9(8), Non-Debt Instruments Rules, 2019. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Vijay Karia v. Prysmian (2020): Mere FEMA violations are generally curable; however, non-curable violations causing loss 
of foreign exchange affect fundamental policy of India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Gemini Bay Transcription v. Integrated Sales Service (2022): Enforcement is the rule unless refusal squarely falls under s48. 
201 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč LMJ International v. Sleepwell Industries (2019): Limited scope of inquiry at enforcement stage. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA Violations and Valuation Fraud 
‚ěĘ‚Äč While it normally would not interfere with valuation methods agreed under the SHA, such deference ceases where 
valuation violates statutory FEMA regulations. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under FEMA, any transfer of shares must be at fair market value, determined using recognised methods such as the 
Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) method BUT HERE 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The valuation adopted by Aapico Group violated Section 16(1)(iii) of the Transfer Regulations. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Had supplied incorrect and misleading financial information to the valuer (FTI/FIZ). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Orchestrated losses in step-down subsidiaries, pushed them into liquidation, and thereafter acquired 
assets at throwaway prices. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The valuation was therefore not merely erroneous, but fraudulent and engineered. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Pledge of Shares Without RBI Approval 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ABT Auto had pledged its 50.01% shares in SGAH in favour of Aapico Group BUT under Regulation 18 of the 
Transfer Regulations (as amended on 29.12.2014), a pledge in favour of an overseas lender is permitted only if the 
lender is regulated and supervised as a bank + step-down subsidiaries must obtain prior RBI approval for pledging 
shares. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
No such approval was obtained. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč While procedural FEMA violations are ordinarily curable, when such violations are intertwined with fraud and 
result in loss of foreign exchange, they become non-curable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Loss to the Indian Exchequer 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Due to undervaluation of shares, fraudulent structuring, and illegal enforcement of the pledge, the Petitioners 
caused a loss of approximately INR 822 crores to the Indian exchequer.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Such loss directly affected India‚Äôs economic interest, thereby breaching the fundamental policy of Indian law. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Suppression of Material Facts and Fraud 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SACL had borrowed INR 22,353 lakhs from Kotak Mahindra Bank, whose sanction letter dated 11 September 
2018 mandated prior permission for any change in shareholding or directorship ‚Üí approved by SACL‚Äôs board. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Despite this, Aapico Group effected complete changes in management and shareholding. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This material fact was suppressed before the SIAC tribunal. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Further, Aapico Group, while acting as a JV partner, colluded with foreign executives to acquire step-down subsidiaries 
abroad, thereby committing fraud squarely falling under Section 48(2)(b). 
 
Conclusion: MHC refused enforcement of the foreign arbitral award under Section 48 of the Arbitration Act, holding that: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Violation of FEMA regulations coupled with commission of fraud renders such violations non-curable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The valuation of SGAH shares was not at fair market value and was based on misrepresentation and suppression. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The illegal pledge and enforcement caused substantial loss of foreign exchange. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Award offended the fundamental policy of Indian law and basic notions of justice. 
 
IMAX Corporation v. E-City Entertainment Pvt. Ltd. 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IMAX, a foreign entity, held foreign arbitral awards passed in its favour against E-City, an Indian company ‚Üí  dispute arose 
out of a series of Master Lease Agreements entered into between the parties. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč IMAX and E-City contemplated the opening, development, and operation of 20 IMAX theatres in India, pursuant 
to which E-City initially committed to lease six IMAX systems from IMAX.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The agreements involved multiple financial obligations, including payments for technology, consultancy, and other 
services sourced from outside India. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Enforcement was resisted by E-City on a limited ground relating exclusively to FEMA compliance. 
202 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Single Judge of the High Court refused enforcement, holding that the Master Agreements were void and unenforceable 
due to non-compliance with FEMA and the FEMA Current Account Transaction Rules, 2000, since prior approval of the 
RBI had allegedly not been obtained. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IMAX appealed before the Division Bench, challenging the refusal to enforce the award. 
 
Issues: Whether the foreign arbitral award violated the public policy of India under Section 48(2)(b) of the Arbitration and 
Conciliation Act, 1996, on the ground that the Master Agreement required prior RBI approval under FEMA, which was allegedly 
not obtained. 
 
Rules: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 48(2)(b), Arbitration and Conciliation Act, 1996 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Enforcement of a foreign award may be refused if it is contrary to the public policy of India, including 
fundamental policy of Indian law, interests of India, justice or morality 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Foreign Exchange Management Act, 1999 (FEMA) ‚Üí regulates foreign exchange transactions with a civil, regulatory, and 
facilitative framework, unlike its predecessor FERA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Foreign Exchange Management (Current Account Transactions) Rules, 2000 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rule 4: Prior approval of the Government of India is required for transactions listed in Schedule II. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Rule 5: Prior approval of RBI is required for transactions listed in Schedule III, subject to exceptions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Schedule III, Item 8: Covers ‚Äúremittance for use and/or purchase of trademark / franchise in India.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Vijay Karia v. Prysmian (2020): mere violation of FEMA is generally curable ‚Üí only non-curable violations causing loss of 
foreign exchange or affecting the economy may attract public policy concerns. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Single Judge held that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Master Agreement violated FEMA, 1999 and the FEMA Current Account Transaction Rules, 2000. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The agreement contemplated: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Consultancy services procured from outside India 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Remittances exceeding USD 100,000 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Royalty and lump-sum payments under unregistered technical collaboration agreements 
‚ěĘ‚Äč These fell under Rule 5 read with Schedule III, which required prior RBI approval. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Since such approval was not obtained, the Master Agreement was contrary to FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On enforcement, the Single Judge held that: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč RBI approval was a mandatory and preceding condition for execution of the Agreement for Lease ‚Üí violation was 
not curable in nature BECAUSE any remittance without approval would impact the Indian economy and affect the 
public interest at large. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, enforcement of the award would amount to a breach of the fundamental policy of Indian law, justifying 
refusal under Section 48(2)(b). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Division Bench reversed the Single Judge, taking a markedly pro-enforcement and FEMA-consistent approach. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A plain reading of the Master Agreement showed that none of the transactions were prohibited under FEMA or 
the 2000 Rules. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
At most, they might have required RBI approval, which the parties had even attempted to obtain. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Even if prior RBI approval was required and not obtained, does not render the Master Agreement void and nor 
does it invalidate the agreement as originally entered into or subsequently restructured ‚Üí reflects the fundamental 
distinction between FEMA and FERA: FEMA is regulatory and facilitative, not prohibitory. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Contracts are not void merely due to regulatory non-compliance. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A mere violation of FEMA provisions, by itself, does not lead to the conclusion that enforcement of a foreign award is 
contrary to public policy. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 48 demands a high threshold, and enforcement cannot be refused for technical or procedural infractions. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Single Judge erred in treating regulatory non-approval as a fundamental illegality. 
203 
 
 
Conclusion:The Division Bench allowed enforcement of the foreign arbitral award, holding that: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Master Agreement did not contemplate prohibited transactions under FEMA. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč At most, the transactions required RBI approval, absence of which does not void the contract. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Mere FEMA violations, especially procedural ones, do not attract Section 48(2)(b). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Enforcement of the award was not contrary to the public policy of India. 
 
Session 8 
IDBI Trusteeship Services Limited v Hubtown Limited [2016 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A Netherlands-based development finance institution, FMO, invested ‚āĻ418 crores in an Indian company, Vinca Developers 
Pvt Ltd, by subscribing to equity shares (10%) and CCDs. Upon conversion of the CCDs, FMO would acquire 99% of 
Vinca‚Äôs equity, though such conversion had not yet occurred. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Under a set of interlinked agreements, Vinca was required to immediately deploy the funds received from FMO to subscribe 
to OPCDs issued by two Indian companies, Amazia Developers Pvt Ltd and Rubix Trading, engaged in township and real 
estate development ‚Üí carried a fixed return of 14.5% per annum. IDBI Trusteeship Services Ltd was appointed as the 
debenture trustee for this transaction. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Hubtown Ltd, the parent company of Vinca, executed an unconditional and irrevocable corporate guarantee in favour of 
IDBI, securing repayment of the OPCDs issued by Amazia and Rubix to Vinca. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč When Amazia and Rubix defaulted on interest payments, IDBI invoked the guarantee and filed a summary suit to enforce it. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč BHC accepted Hubtown‚Äôs argument that the entire investment structure was a colourable device designed to do indirectly 
what FEMA and the FDI policy prohibit directly.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč By interposing Vinca as an Indian company, FMO was able to route its investment into OPCDs carrying a fixed 
return, something it could not have done directly in the real estate sector.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The corporate guarantee was treated as an integral part of this allegedly illegal scheme, intended to secure assured 
returns for FMO.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč On this reasoning, the guarantee was held to be unenforceable as being contrary to law and public policy, and the 
doctrine of pari delicto was applied to deny relief. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the transaction structure was illegal or unenforceable as being contrary to FEMA and the FDI policy, on the 
ground that it enabled a foreign investor to secure assured returns in the real estate sector indirectly. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether, assuming such illegality, the Court could refuse enforcement of the corporate guarantee by applying the doctrine 
of pari delicto, as articulated in Immami Appa Rao v G Ramalingamurthi. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Hubtown had raised a substantial defence sufficient to warrant leave to defend in a summary suit. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Under FEMA and the prevailing FDI policy, foreign investment in the construction and real estate development sector is 
permitted only through equity instruments, without any assured or fixed return. Debt instruments or structures 
guaranteeing fixed returns to foreign investors are prohibited. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč At the same time, FEMA regulates cross-border foreign exchange transactions, and not purely domestic transfers. A 
contravention arises only when there is an unauthorised inflow or outflow of foreign exchange. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The doctrine of pari delicto prevents courts from enforcing illegal agreements where both parties are equally at fault.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč However, as held in Immami Appa Rao, the doctrine applies only when the illegal object has been fully carried out, 
not where illegality is merely contemplated or executory. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The transaction under scrutiny was not the foreign investment itself, but the enforcement of an unconditional corporate 
guarantee between Indian parties.  
204 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The initial investment of ‚āĻ418 crores by FMO into Vinca through equity and CCDs was not, by itself, alleged to 
violate FEMA.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč More importantly, enforcement of the guarantee involved payment by Hubtown to IDBI, an Indian debenture 
trustee, for the benefit of Vinca, another Indian company. At this stage, there was no foreign exchange outflow. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč REJECTED the argument that FEMA was violated merely because the broader commercial arrangement contemplated 
assured returns for FMO ‚Üí FEMA scrutiny would arise only when FMO actually acquired control of Vinca or sought to 
repatriate funds outside India.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč At that later stage, RBI permission would be required, and if such permission were refused, no FEMA violation 
would occur. Thus, regulatory control was deferred, not defeated. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On the application of Immami Appa Rao, the Court clarified that the doctrine of pari delicto applies only where the illegal 
purpose has been fully carried out.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč In the present case, the alleged illegality [foreign investor securing assured returns and repatriating them] had not 
yet materialised ‚Üí the agreements were, at most, executory in this respect ‚Üí refused to deny relief at the threshold. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč While the Court acknowledged that Hubtown had raised a defence based on FEMA and FDI policy, it characterised this 
defence as ‚Äúplausible but improbable‚ÄĚ.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Given the magnitude of the claim and the unconditional nature of the guarantee, leave to defend was granted only 
on the condition that Hubtown deposit or secure the principal sum of ‚āĻ418 crores. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč At the present stage, enforcement of the corporate guarantee did not violate FEMA or the FDI policy, since no prohibited 
foreign exchange transaction had occurred ‚Üí the alleged illegality was merely contemplated and not executed ‚áí the 
doctrine of pari delicto was therefore inapplicable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Accordingly, the guarantee was not rendered unenforceable, though the defendant was granted conditional leave to defend 
upon securing the principal amount. 
 
Suborno Bose v. Enforcement Directorate [2020 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Foreign exchange remittance made in 2000 by Zoom Enterprises Ltd., prior to Suborno Bose becoming its MD ‚Üí remitted 
FF 3,74,000 and USD 35,766 to foreign suppliers under the EPCG scheme for import of HVAC equipment ‚Üí the goods 
reached India, kept in a bonded warehouse and neither cleared nor supported by submission of the Bill of Entry to the 
authorised dealer. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No steps were taken to complete the import or surrender the unused foreign exchange, leading to proceedings for 
contravention of FEMA and the applicable RBI regulations and manuals.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč After assuming office on 22 October 2001, Bose became aware of these defaults but failed to regularise the transaction 
despite seeking extensions, resulting in enforcement action against him in his capacity as MD. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč ED issued a show cause notice alleging contravention of s.10(6) of FEMA, read with ss.46 and 47, paragraphs A-10 and A-11 
of the Foreign Exchange Manual (2003‚Äď04), and Regulation 6 of the FEMA (Realisation, Repatriation and Surrender of 
Foreign Exchange) Regulations, 2000.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Proceedings were initiated not only against the Company but also against Suborno Bose personally under s42 of 
FEMA, which provides for vicarious liability. 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether Suborno Bose could be held personally liable for contraventions under FEMA that originated prior to his 
assumption of office as MD, particularly when the alleged violations were continuing in nature. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether failure to rectify or regularise an earlier FEMA contravention attracts liability under Section 10(6), read with the 
relevant regulations, even if the director was not in charge of the company at the time the initial remittance was made. 
 
Rule: 
205 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 10(6) of the FEMA Act ‚Äď deeming provision which treats certain conduct as a contravention of FEMA ‚Äď three 
independent circumstances:  
‚ěĘ‚Äč first, where foreign exchange is acquired or purchased for a declared purpose but not used for that purpose;  
‚ěĘ‚Äč second, where the foreign exchange so acquired is not surrendered to the authorised person within the specified 
period; and  
‚ěĘ‚Äč third, where foreign exchange is used for a purpose not permissible under FEMA or the rules and directions framed 
thereunder. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Regulation 6 of the FEMA (Realisation, Repatriation and Surrender of Foreign Exchange) Regulations, 2000 mandates 
surrender of unused foreign exchange within sixty days from the date of acquisition.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Additionally, for imports exceeding USD 5,000, submission of the exchange control copy of the Bill of Entry to the 
authorised dealer is mandatory. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 42 of FEMA ‚Üí vicarious liability ‚Üí every person who was in charge of and responsible for the conduct of the 
business of the company liable for contraventions, unless such person proves that the contravention took place without his 
knowledge or that he exercised all due diligence to prevent it. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Contraventions under FEMA, particularly those involving non-use or non-surrender of foreign exchange, may constitute 
continuing offences until rectified. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Although the goods had physically reached India, the import transaction was never completed because the Bill of Entry was 
not submitted and the goods were not cleared from the bonded warehouse ‚áí the foreign exchange remitted was not used 
for the purpose for which it was acquired ‚Üí attracted Section 10(6)(i). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Company also failed to surrender the unused foreign exchange within the period stipulated under Regulation 6, 
thereby independently attracting Section 10(6)(ii).  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Compliance with Regulation 6 is not optional, and expiry of the prescribed period does not extinguish the 
contravention ‚Üí once the period expires, the contravention becomes a deemed and continuing contravention. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A contravention under Section 10(6) is a continuing actionable offense ‚Üí as long as the imported goods remained uncleared 
and the statutory obligations were not discharged, the contravention continued to subsist ‚áí every person who assumed 
control of the Company while the contravention was ongoing bore a duty to take corrective steps in right earnest. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč HERE ‚Üí Suborno Bose had taken over management in October 2001 and was fully aware of the default.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Instead of rectifying the contravention, he sought indulgence and extensions from authorities ‚Üí proviso to s.42(1) 
was therefore unavailable to him, as he neither pleaded nor proved lack of knowledge or due diligence. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FEMA creates civil liability and does not require proof of mens rea.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The obligation is regulatory and remedial in nature. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč As long as the default continues, the persons in charge remain liable, irrespective of when the default first arose. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Upheld the proceedings against Suborno Bose and affirmed that he was personally liable under s.42 of FEMA for the 
continuing contravention committed by the Company.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Contraventions under s.10(6) are continuing offences, and liability attaches to every person managing the affairs of the 
company until effective corrective steps are taken. 
 
 
Swarup v ED [2020 | SC | 2 J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč MXL Ltd. made twenty foreign exchange remittances from 1985 onwards through Standard Chartered Bank ‚Üí RBI later 
reported that MXL failed to submit exchange control copies of Bills of Entry or other import evidence despite reminders.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč During investigations between 1991 and 1993, MXL produced documents for four transactions and CA certificates for the 
remaining sixteen, citing non-availability of old records. MXL subsequently merged into XMC Ltd. 
206 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A show cause notice under FERA proposed proceedings against the company and its directors, including Swarup, under 
Sections 8 and 68.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Swarup replied that he was only a part-time, non-executive Director with no role in day-to-day management, a 
claim supported by the Company Secretary‚Äôs affidavit.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Nonetheless, penalties were imposed on him personally, which were upheld by the Appellate Tribunal and the 
High Court. 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the AP‚Äôs plea that he was a part-time, non-executive Director not responsible for the conduct of the business of the 
company was liable to be rejected as an afterthought merely because such a plea had not been expressly taken in an earlier 
reply. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether proceedings under Section 68 of FERA could be sustained against a director in the absence of any specific finding 
or material showing that he was in charge of and responsible for the conduct of the business of the company at the time of 
commission of the contravention. 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.68 of FERA, 1973 ‚Üí every person who, at the time the contravention was committed, was in charge of and responsible to 
the company for the conduct of its business shall be deemed to be guilty of the contravention. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The adjudicating authority, while acting under s.51 of FERA, is under a statutory obligation to hold an inquiry, consider 
representations, and return a clear finding that the person proceeded against satisfies the requirements of s.68. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč First, SC rejected the HC‚Äôs conclusion that Swarup‚Äôs defence was an afterthought. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč  The affidavit of the Company Secretary dated 4 July 2003, which clearly stated that the AP was only a part-time, 
non-executive Director and not responsible for day-to-day affairs, was in fact on record and had been erroneously 
ignored ‚Üí no material produced by the ED to rebut this assertion or to establish that Swarup exercised managerial 
or operational control over the company at the relevant time. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Secondly, none of the authorities had recorded any specific finding that the AP was in charge of and responsible for the 
conduct of the company‚Äôs business when the alleged contraventions under Sections 8(3) and 8(4) occurred ‚Üí the penalty 
had been imposed solely on the basis that he was a director, which is impermissible under Section 68. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Vicarious liability is an exception to the general rule against penal liability for another‚Äôs acts and must therefore be strictly 
construed.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The statutory conditions extending liability to directors are intended to ensure that only those who had a real 
connection with the contravention are proceeded against.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč In the absence of specific averments and findings as to responsibility and control, proceedings against a director 
cannot be sustained. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč HERE, Swarup was neither shown to be an executive director nor involved in the management of the company ‚Üí mere 
association with the board, without evidence of control or responsibility, was held insufficient to attract Section 68. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Allowed the appeal and set aside the penalty imposed on the AP ‚Üí proceedings under Section 68 of FERA against Swarup 
were unsustainable, as there was no material or finding to establish that he was in charge of and responsible for the conduct 
of the business of the company at the time of the alleged contravention. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Vicarious liability under FERA depends on functional responsibility, not formal designation, and that part-time, 
non-executive directors cannot be mechanically proceeded against in the absence of concrete evidence of control.
